moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, immediately claiming central space and opening the diagonal for the dark-squared bishop. Unlike 1.e4, this move creates a solid pawn chain that's harder to undermine.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Single d4 pawn controls e5 and c5. White aims to expand with c4 next.
plans: Follow up with c4 to build an ideal pawn center
Develop pieces behind the pawn chain for a strong middlegame

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black fights for the center immediately by meeting d4 with d5. This is the most principled response — Black stakes an equal claim in the center and prevents White from getting a two-pawn ideal cente.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Symmetrical d4 vs d5. The central tension will define the opening battle.
plans: Maintain the d5 pawn and develop pieces naturally
Look for ...c5 to challenge White's center when the time is right
alternatives: 1...Nf6 (Indian Defenses) — flexible, avoids committing the d-pawn early

moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the Queen's Gambit — White offers the c4 pawn to lure Black's d5 pawn away from the center. It's not a real gambit because Black can't hold the pawn comfortably.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White has pawns on d4 and c4, pressuring Black's d5 from the side.
plans: If Black takes on c4, recapture or play e4 for a big center
If Black holds d5, build pressure with Nc3 and prepare to exchange on d5
alternatives: 2.Nf3 — solid development, keeps options open

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black declines the gambit and supports d5 solidly with e6. This is the Queen's Gambit Declined — the most classical and reliable response.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's pawn chain d5-e6 is solid but passive. The c8 bishop is blocked.
plans: Develop pieces and maintain the solid d5-e6 structure
Eventually break with ...c5 or ...e5 to free the position
alternatives: 2...dxc4 (Queen's Gambit Accepted) — gives up the center but gains time

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to its best square, adding another attacker to d5. Now the c4 pawn and the c3 knight both pressure d5, and Black has to decide how to handle this tension.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's c4+d4 vs Black's d5+e6. The pressure on d5 is mounting with two pieces and a pawn targetin.
plans: Increase pressure on d5 and prepare cxd5 at the right moment
Prepare e4 after the d5 tension resolves in White's favor

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to its most natural square, defending d5 and controlling e4. This is standard developing chess — every piece should go to its best square.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The d5-e6 chain remains solid. Black's minor pieces are developing naturally.
plans: Continue development with ...Be7 and ...O-O
Maintain d5 and look for counterplay with ...c5 or ...dxc4

moveAnnotations.annotation: White captures on d5, entering the Exchange Variation of the QGD. This is a strategic decision — by exchanging pawns, White creates a symmetrical pawn structure but with a key advantage: the minorit.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: After the exchange, White has a d4 pawn vs Black's e6.
plans: Launch the minority attack with b4-b5 to create queenside weaknesses
Develop the dark-squared bishop actively before playing e3
alternatives: 5.Bg5 (Classical) — maintains the tension and pins the knight

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black recaptures with the e-pawn, which is virtually forced. Taking with the knight would lose central presence. Now the pawn structure is symmetrical — both sides have a d-pawn supported by pieces.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Symmetrical d4 vs d5 with open e-file. Black's d5 pawn is the long-term target — it can become wea.
plans: Develop the light bishop actively to f5 or g4 before White restricts it
Use the open e-file and aim for piece activity to compensate for the isolated...

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop aggressively before blocking it in with e3. This is the most precise move order — you always want to develop the dark-squared bishop BEFORE playing e3, because e3 locks it.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Symmetrical d4/d5. The bishop on g5 adds pressure to the kingside and the d5 pawn indirectly by ta.
plans: Maintain the pin on f6 to pressure Black's d5 pawn
If Black plays ...Be7, exchange on e7 to weaken Black's kingside or double pawns

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7, which is the most natural and solid response to the Bg5 pin. The bishop unpins the knight and prepares castling.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Structure unchanged. Black is focused on completing development and getting the king to safety.
plans: Castle kingside immediately
Consider ...Nbd7 to reroute the knight and support the center
alternatives: 6...h6 — forces the bishop to declare its intentions but weakens the kingside...

moveAnnotations.annotation: Now that the dark-squared bishop is already developed to g5, White plays e3 to solidify the d4 pawn and open the diagonal for the light-squared bishop.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's e3-d4 chain is very solid. The light-squared bishop will develop to d3, targeting the king.
plans: Develop the light bishop to d3 aiming at h7
Castle kingside and then start the minority attack with Rab1 and b4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles kingside, getting the king to safety and connecting the rooks. This is the right time to castle — the bishop is developed, the knight is on f6, and there's nothing more urgent.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Structure unchanged. The king is safe on g8 with the f7-g7-h7 pawn shield intact.
plans: Develop the remaining minor pieces with ...Nbd7 and ...Re8
Prepare ...c6 to support d5 and deny White's knight access to b5

moveAnnotations.annotation: White places the bishop on its ideal diagonal, pointing straight at h7. The Bd3 is a monster in the Exchange QGD — it supports a potential kingside attack and eyes the h7 pawn, which is a classic ta.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's e3-d4 provides a solid base for the bishop.
plans: Play Qc2 to create the battery on the b1-h7 diagonal
Combine the kingside battery with the queenside minority attack for pressure ...

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the last minor piece to d7, where it supports the knight on f6 and keeps options flexible.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Structure unchanged. Black is completing development before committing to a plan.
plans: Play ...c6 to support d5 and create a solid queenside structure
Reroute the knight via Nf8-e6 or Nb6 depending on White's setup
alternatives: 8...c6 — immediately supporting d5, but the knight still needs to develop

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the kingside knight to its natural square, controlling e5 and preparing to castle. The knight on f3 is perfectly placed — it supports d4, eyes e5, and helps with kingside castling.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Structure unchanged. White's setup is nearly complete — just needs to castle and start the minorit.
plans: Castle kingside next to complete development
Then play Rab1, a3, and b4 to begin the minority attack

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black places the rook on the semi-open e-file, which is the most logical square for it. The rook on e8 eyes the e3 pawn and supports a potential ...e5 break in the future.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Structure unchanged. The open e-file becomes a point of contention between both sides.
plans: Prepare ...Nf8-e6 to challenge the bishop on g5
Keep the option of ...e5 alive in the right circumstances

moveAnnotations.annotation: White completes the famous Bd3+Qc2 battery on the b1-h7 diagonal. This is one of the most important setups in the Exchange QGD.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Structure unchanged. The Bd3+Qc2 battery creates latent threats against Black's kingside.
plans: Castle and then launch the minority attack with Rab1, a3, b4-b5
Keep the kingside threats alive — if Black plays ...g6, the battery becomes l...

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays the critical move ...c6, solidifying the d5 pawn once and for all. This is Black's main defensive idea in the Exchange QGD — the c6 pawn makes d5 rock-solid and also prevents any Nb5 ideas.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black has the c6-d5 pawn chain. This is very solid but slightly passive.
plans: Hold the solid position and look for piece activity
Consider ...Ne4 to exchange pieces and ease the pressure
alternatives: 10...Nf8 — rerouting the knight for kingside defense first
